Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd go through intense periods where I'd barely study at all, then suddenly cram everything in when I realized I was falling behind. Being 28 and having been out of school for a while, I struggled to find a consistent rhythm. I tried to listen to lectures during commutes or while doing other things since I learn best by hearing information. Most weeks I'd manage about 15 hours, but it was never evenly distributed.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I rarely had regular study times because anxiety would sometimes paralyze me for days, then I'd have to catch up frantically. When I felt scared about failing, I'd suddenly find hours I didn't know I had. The inconsistency stressed me out even more, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle no matter how hard I tried.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment or realize I hadn't checked in for a while and feel this wave of fear wash over me. Sometimes I'd get a notification from the peer support group, and that would remind me to check in. Other times it was just guilt eating at me until I finally opened the platform to see what I'd missed.
Q4: For a brief period when I connected with a couple of peers through the support program, logging in became more routine because I wanted to see if they'd responded to my messages. But that didn't last long because my anxiety made me pull back from those interactions. I never really developed a consistent habit with the LMS because every time I logged in, I'd see how much I'd fallen behind and it would terrify me.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner and I could actually absorb information that way. I watched over 113 minutes of video content because hearing explanations really helped things click for me. The peer support feature was helpful when I actually used it, even though I only posted once in discussions. Having someone else going through similar struggles made me feel less alone in my fear.
Q6: I largely ignored the discussion forums because the idea of posting publicly made my anxiety spike. I also delayed reading materials because staring at text doesn't work well for my learning style. Some of the supplementary resources I just never touched because I was already overwhelmed trying to keep up with the core content. When you're scared and barely holding on, optional readings feel impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could listen to it or if it directly related to an upcoming exam or assignment. Anything that I knew would improve my grade felt essential because my fear of failing drove most of my decisions. I also valued activities that didn't require me to interact publicly with others since that triggered my anxiety. If something could be done privately at my own pace, I was more likely to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was constantly in crisis mode just trying to complete the required work. Required activities carried this weight of fear attached to them, which ironically sometimes made me avoid them longer. With optional stuff, there was no consequence for skipping it, so my anxious brain would deprioritize it immediately. The pressure of requirements both motivated and paralyzed me depending on the day.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded audio versions of lectures and listened to them repeatedly on my phone while doing other things. I'd record myself summarizing key concepts and play those back because hearing information in my own voice helped cement it. A lot of my studying happened through podcasts and YouTube videos on related topics that wouldn't show up in any LMS log. My best learning often happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be lying in bed listening to downloaded lecture recordings on repeat, trying to absorb the material through pure audio repetition. I'd also have study conversations with the few peers I connected with through text messages, working through concepts verbally. Sometimes I'd pace around my apartment talking through problems out loud to myself. None of that shows up anywhere, but that's genuinely how I learned best.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture video once, convert it to audio, and then listen to it maybe ten or fifteen times over the following weeks. My LMS would show one view, but the actual engagement was exponentially higher. This was my main strategy for exam preparation, which is probably why I scored well on exams despite my terrible attendance and assignment completion rates.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety became so overwhelming that I couldn't even open the LMS without feeling sick. When I saw how far behind I'd fallen, the fear would become paralyzing rather than motivating. Life stuff would pile up, and my low resilience meant I'd crumble pretty quickly under pressure. Those dark periods could last days or even weeks where I'd just avoid everything course-related entirely.
Q13: The workload felt crushing when combined with my anxiety, and confusion about what to prioritize made everything worse. When I was fatigued, my brain just couldn't process written information at all, so I'd have to wait until I could listen to something instead. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage because I'd often choose to deal with immediate life problems rather than face the fear of logging in. My engagement was directly tied to my mental state on any given day.
Q14: Many times I'd log in and just scroll through pages without actually absorbing anything because my mind was racing with anxiety. I'd have videos playing in the background while I was too distracted by worry to actually listen. Sometimes I'd open reading materials and stare at them while my thoughts spiraled about all the work I hadn't done. The LMS might show activity, but my brain was completely elsewhere.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important, which is why my completion rate was so low. The fear of not doing them perfectly made starting feel impossible, so I'd avoid them until the last possible moment or miss them entirely. It's this terrible cycle where the anxiety about the work prevents you from doing the work, which creates more anxiety. I knew this pattern was hurting me, but knowing didn't help me stop it.
Q16: Deadlines were both my worst enemy and my only motivator. I'd ignore everything until a deadline loomed close enough to trigger full panic mode, then I'd cram desperately. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all because the fear of consequences was the only thing that could override my avoidance instincts.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me because receiving it triggered intense anxiety about my performance. When feedback was positive, it gave me a brief boost of confidence that might last a day or two. Negative feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for a while. I needed feedback to improve, but I was also scared to receive it, which created this frustrating push-pull dynamic.
Q18: The course structure with its many components felt overwhelming to me because I couldn't figure out what to prioritize. Having 19 online courses worth of material meant I was constantly behind on something, which fed my anxiety. The peer support intervention helped somewhat because it gave me human connection, but the overall structure didn't accommodate someone with my mental health challenges. I needed more flexibility and clearer guidance on what was essential versus optional.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my auditory learning style. However, the emphasis on discussion posts and written assignments made engagement harder because those formats don't work well for me. The lack of clear priority indicators made everything feel equally urgent and important, which paralyzed me. Having more audio-based assessment options would have helped me demonstrate my actual understanding.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I'd log in frequently out of anxiety, watch videos, scroll through materials, but then be too scared or overwhelmed to actually submit work. The fear of submitting something imperfect was often greater than the fear of submitting nothing. Sometimes high activity is just someone desperately trying to catch up or figure out what they've missed while being too paralyzed to actually produce anything.
Q21: That also partially describes me because my exam score was quite good despite my low attendance and assignment completion. I learned most effectively through downloaded audio materials and offline study methods that never showed up in the LMS. Someone might look at my low activity numbers and assume I wasn't learning, but I was actually putting in significant effort just not in ways the system could track. Performance doesn't always correlate with visible online engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone logs in and what they click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if someone is actually absorbing information or just going through the motions while anxious and distracted. They miss all offline learning, all audio-based study, all the mental processing that happens away from the screen. The emotional state behind every interaction is invisible to the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to check boxes. For me, it's when I'm actively listening and thinking about concepts, making connections, talking through ideas out loud. It's when the fear subsides enough that I can actually focus on learning rather than just surviving. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, which can happen completely offline and invisibly.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how much I actually learned through audio repetition of downloaded materials. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happened through text messages or the emotional toll that every interaction with the course took on me. It can't show that my low visible engagement was often because I was too scared to log in, not because I didn't care. My anxiety, my fear, my struggle to keep going despite everything—none of that appears in any log.
